coredata.dc:title: Chitosan modified magnetic nanocomposite for biofilm destruction and precise photothermal/photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Yumeng
Wu, Jing
Shen, Jian
Xu, Yan
Li, Lu
Wang, Wentao
Zhou, Ninglin
Zhang, Ming
coredata.dc:description: Getting rid of the biofilms is a major challenge when treating skin and soft tissue infections (SSTI), an inflammatory illness brought on by bacteria. Traditional magnetic materials have a limited dispersibility and a biofilm permeable property, making it challenging to remove biofilms and causing infection to linger. To solve these problems, we developed a kind of magnetic composite nanoplatform coated with indocyanine green carbon dots and modified with chitosan modification (Fe-ICGCDs@CS). Fe-ICGCDs@CS has high dispersibility and improves the conductivity of biofilms under magnetic action. Fe-ICGCDs@CS can adsorb bacteria via the positive charge and achieve precise photothermal sterilization and photodynamic therapy (PDT). Moreover, by catalyzing hydrogen peroxide (2 mM), Fe-ICGCDs@CS can produce oxygen to relieve the anoxic state in the deep layer of biofilms and activate dormant bacteria to make them sensitive to external stimuli. All in all, unlike the common “just kill” sterilization model, Fe-ICGCDs@CS can accurately kill bacteria and be recovered by an external magnetic field at the end of treatment, thus reducing the potential biological toxicity of nanomaterials. Therefore, the proposed Fe-ICGCDs@CS provides a new antibacterial method with low biotoxicity for clinical application in the treatment of biofilm infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacteria adsorption
Photothermal
Magnetic
Photodynamic therapy
Oxygen production
Biofilm scavenging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Jiangsu Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Function Materials
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials and Jiangsu Province Engineering Research Center of Stomatological Translational Medicine
Key Project of the Natural Science Foundation for Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the financial support from the Key Project of the Natural Science Foundation for Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BE2020347 ), Jiangsu Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Function Materials , Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials , and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials and Jiangsu Province Engineering Research Center of Stomatological Translational Medicine .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagrammatic representation and mechanism of Fe-ICGCDs@CS preparation for synergistic antibacterial.
A) TEM image of ICGCDs. B) HRTEM image of ICGCDs. C) SEM image of Fe-ICGCDs. D) High magnification SEM image of Fe-ICGCDs@CS. E) Element mappings and EDX spectrum of Fe-ICGCDs@CS.

A) FTIR spectra of different nanocomposites. B) UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra of different nanocomposites. C) XRD spectra of different nanocomposites and standard spectrum of Fe3O4. D) XPS high-resolution scans of Fe-ICGCDs@CS. E) The high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p. F) Magnetic hysteresis curve of Fe-ICGCDs@CS at 300 K.

A) Photothermal heating curves and B) thermal images of Fe-ICGCDs@CS solutions at various concentrations. C) Temperature changing curves for Fe-ICGCDs@CS solutions at varying irradiation power densities. D) Temperature curves of Fe-ICGCDs@CS dispersion (400 μg/mL) under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2) for five “ON/OFF” cycles. E) Temperature profiles of Fe-ICGCDs@CS (400 μg/mL) distributed in water irradiated by 808 nm (1.5 W/cm2) when the light was switched off and followed by natural cooling.

A) Absorption spectra of DPBF with 808 nm irradiation. B) Absorption curves of DPBF in the presence of the Fe-ICGCDs@CS (400 μg/mL). C) Absorption curves of DPBF in the presence of the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2). D) ESR spectra of 1O2 induced by different nanocomposites under 808 nm laser (1.5 W/cm2, 10 min). E) Plot of the number of O2 bubbles with different incubations within 50 min. F) Microscopic images of O2 bubbles with Fe-ICGCDs@CS (400 μg/mL) treatment with different concentrations of H2O2 substrate.
The fluorescence images of A) E. coli and B) S. aureus with different incubations for 12 h.

A) ROS detection in bacteria using the DCFH-DA probe (40 μM). B) Colonies formation of E. coli and S. aureus after different treatments. C) Live/dead staining analysis of E. coli and S. aureus after different treatments.
A) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus with different treatments. TEM images of B) E. coli and C) S. aureus after various treatments. D) CLSM images of E. coli and S. aureus formed biofilms.

A) In vivo antibacterial activity and healing effect of Fe-ICGCDs@CS for S. aureus-infected chronic wound. B) Thermal images of chronic trauma mice exposed to 808 nm laser irradiation at 0 and 10 min after PBS and Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR treatments. C) Representative photographs of treated chronic wounds. D) Quantitative analysis of the remaining treated wound sites. Error bars showed the mean ± SD; n = 3, *p < 0.05 and ***p < 0.001.
A) Traces of wound-bed closure after 9 days. B) H&E and C) Masson staining of the wound tissue. Immunohistochemistry analysis of D) CD31 and E) TNF-α in wound tissues after different treatments.

head.ce:title: Chitosan modified magnetic nanocomposite for biofilm destruction and precise photothermal/photodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases, Jiangsu Province Engineering Research Center of Stomatological Translational Medicine, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing 210029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases
Jiangsu Province Engineering Research Center of Stomatological Translational Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases, Jiangsu Province Engineering Research Center of Stomatological Translational Medicine, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing 210029, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biomedical Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biomedical Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biomedical Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Getting rid of the biofilms is a major challenge when treating skin and soft tissue infections (SSTI), an inflammatory illness brought on by bacteria. Traditional magnetic materials have a limited dispersibility and a biofilm permeable property, making it challenging to remove biofilms and causing infection to linger. To solve these problems, we developed a kind of magnetic composite nanoplatform coated with indocyanine green carbon dots and modified with chitosan modification (Fe-ICGCDs@CS). Fe-ICGCDs@CS has high dispersibility and improves the conductivity of biofilms under magnetic action. Fe-ICGCDs@CS can adsorb bacteria via the positive charge and achieve precise photothermal sterilization and photodynamic therapy (PDT). Moreover, by catalyzing hydrogen peroxide (2 mM), Fe-ICGCDs@CS can produce oxygen to relieve the anoxic state in the deep layer of biofilms and activate dormant bacteria to make them sensitive to external stimuli. All in all, unlike the common “just kill” sterilization model, Fe-ICGCDs@CS can accurately kill bacteria and be recovered by an external magnetic field at the end of treatment, thus reducing the potential biological toxicity of nanomaterials. Therefore, the proposed Fe-ICGCDs@CS provides a new antibacterial method with low biotoxicity for clinical application in the treatment of biofilm infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin and soft tissue infection (SSTI), an inflammatory condition that negatively affects the physical and mental health of patients, is brought on by pathogenic bacteria infiltrating the epidermis, dermis, and subcutaneous tissues. Unfortunately, the misuse of antibiotics, which is the main treatment for SSTI, has resulted in the emergence of widespread drug-resistant bacteria [1–3]. Therefore, it is crucial to discover alternative techniques that have great antibacterial efficacy and avoid risks of antibiotic resistance. For bacterial infections, a range of materials with effective antibacterial activity has recently been developed, including hydrogels [4–6], antibacterial medicines [7–10], peptides [11,12], noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) [13–16], etc. However, the unsatisfactory biocompatibility and ineffective therapeutic impacts still restrict their application.

Photothermal therapy (PTT) as a type of light-responsive therapy, can raise local temperature under near-infrared light irradiation and has attracted growing interest in antibacterial applications [17–20]. The local hyperthermia effect can effectively kill bacteria through a variety of pathways, including cell membrane rupture, cell hollowing, protein/enzyme degeneration, and fluid evaporation. However, high temperature or protracted exposure to hyperthermia may result in thermal damage to surrounding healthy tissues and trigger inflammation. Therefore, it is critical to develop safer and more effective antibacterial PTT strategies, such as using relatively lower temperatures (around 50 °C) or shortening treatment time. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) with the advantages of highly spatial-temporal selectivity, unique superiorities of noninvasiveness, minimal side effects and broad spectrum has been regarded as a potential antibacterial method [21–25], which employs photosensitizers to destroy bacteria via the light-mediated production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively reported in the biomedical field, because of their outstanding biocompatibility, chemical stability, and exceptional optical qualities [26]. However, the easy aggregation of CDs in solution limits their applications. Moreover, the production of 1O2 during the PDT procedure is severely restricted by the hypoxic bacterial infection microenvironment, and the PDT-induced continuous oxygen (O2) consumption will in turn deteriorate the local hypoxia, causing insufficient antibacterial efficiency [27]. Thus, it is essential to design an ideal carrier for CDs to enhance their dispersion and address the limitations of PDT in anoxic environments. Fortunately, Fe3O4 NPs have attracted extensive attention due to their high specific surface area, high volume ratio, and various activities, including chemical dynamic properties [28,29], photodynamic properties [30], CAT-like activity [31], and magnetic properties [32].

Bacterial biofilms can achieve resistance to antibiotics through a series of mechanisms, including drug efflux, extracellular matrix obstruction, dormant effect, population effect, and so on, preventing the release of antibacterial drugs, reducing the treating effectiveness and prolonging disease [33–35]. The drug-resistant mechanism is intrinsically related to the pathological microenvironment of biofilm hypoxia. Bacteria in the inner layer of biofilms are wrapped in polysaccharides and lack nutrients and O2, which induce a low metabolic rate and dormant bacteria. Conventional antibacterial methods can not completely remove the lining bacteria, which can quickly become reactivated and slow down the rate of wound healing once they get enough nutrients. Recently, Xiu [36] et al. developed a method to combat bacterial biofilm infections by enhancing the hypoxic microenvironment and thereby activating antibiotics through photodynamic therapy. However, although this method is effective in treating biofilm infections, it is still inevitable that bacteria will become resistant when this method is used due to the use of antibiotics. Therefore, there is a need to develop a novel antibacterial material that has strong permeability and can activate the inner bacteria. Fortunately, Fe3O4 in biofilms is effective in disrupting the compact structure of biofilms and reducing the colonization of bacteria. In addition, the excellent CAT-like activity of Fe3O4 can catalyze H2O2 to produce O2, a characteristic that overcomes the anoxic microenvironment.

Inspired by the above characteristics, magnetic Fe3O4 particles loaded with CDs (Fe-ICGCDs) were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method. Chitosan (CS) has the advantages of biodegradability, biocompatibility, and mucosal adhesion, which can modulate the intrinsic antimicrobial effect and thus enhance the effectiveness of antimicrobial agents [37,38]. Moreover, CS can diffuse through cellular barriers and flow across delicate intercellular junctions in epithelial cells. The ability of CS sponges to deliver drugs, proteins, growth factors, minerals, vitamins, and fibroblasts subcutaneously has been demonstrated in studies [39]. Here, to increase the solubility of Fe-ICGCDs, CS was also employed for the passivation, and the compound was named Fe-ICGCDs@CS. Fe-ICGCDs@CS could adsorb bacteria through the positive charge of CS and carry out precise PTT and photodynamic therapy (PDT) against bacteria. It is worth noting that the movement of Fe-ICGCDs@CS through biofilms by controlling the external magnetic field can effectively destroy the tight-structured biofilms, alleviate bacterial colonization at wound sites, and activate dormant bacteria. At the end of treatment, Fe-ICGCDs@CS can also be removed by using an external magnetic field, greatly reducing the biotoxicity of NPs. In summary, this synergistic PTT/PDT strategy aims to create a removable magnetic composite nanoplatform to combat bacterial infections (Fig. 1).

3-[4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), indocyanine green (75 %), iron chloride (≥99.9 %), sodium acetate anhydrous (AR), chitosan (200–800 cP, Mw = 2000-5000 kDa), chloral hydrate (AR, >99.0 %), 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA, ≥97 %), and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, 98 %) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); sodium chloride (NaCl, AR, ≥99.5 %), 30 % hydrogen peroxide (AR), ethylene glycol (AR, ≥99.5 %), acetic acid (AR, ≥99.5 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, ≥96.0 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; yeast extract, tryptone, and agar power were obtained from Solarbio; mouse fibroblast cells (L929), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and Calcein-AM/PI live cell/dead cell double staining kit was purchased from Jiangsu KeyGEN Bio-Tech Corp., Ltd.; SYTO9 green fluorescent nucleic acid stain was obtained from Invitrogen. All chemicals were employed immediately without any depuration. Deionized water was used in all experiments with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm.

Bacterial strains Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) were provided by the Jiangsu Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention. Animal studies were conducted in accordance with the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory. All studies were conducted according to the institutional guidelines of Nanjing Normal University.

The morphologies of Fe-ICGCDs@CS and bacterial cells were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Jem-2100F and EM UC7, Leica, Germany) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS-sigma300 and Tescan-mira4), respectively. The microstructures, functional groups, and chemical compositions of Fe-ICGCDs@CS were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany), Nexus 670 (Thermo Nicolet, US) FTIR thermal spectrophotometer, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), respectively. The hydrodynamic diameters of Fe-ICGCDs@CS were measured using a dynamic light scattering spectrometer (DLS, Malvern Nano-ZS90, UK). UV–vis absorption was measured on a Cary 50 (Agilent, Australia) spectrophotometer, respectively. Fluorescent graphs of cells were imaged via confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) using a TI-E-A1R (Nikon, Japan) instrument. Magnetic measurement of the Fe-ICGCDs@CS (VSM, LakeShore 7404, USA) was studied at room temperature. The measurement of electron spin resonance (ESR) was performed in a Bruker EMX ESR spectrometer (Billerica, MA). Specific characterization methods are shown in the Supplementary Information.

ICG, an imaging dye approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), has a good photothermal effect and strong absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) region [40,41]. However, poor water stability, rapid degradation rate, and non-specific binding to proteins limit its application [42–44]. Fortunately, the photostability of ICGCDs prepared using ICG as a precursor system can be greatly improved [45]. Here, ICGCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. The TEM image shows that ICGCDs had uniform shape and stable size around 4 nm (Fig. 2A). The lattice state of ICGCDs could be clearly seen in the HRTEM image with a 0.21 nm lattice spacing, agreed with previous reports (Fig. 2B). Then, FeCl3, sodium acetate, and ICGCDs were mixed in ethylene glycol, and ICGCDs were loaded on Fe3O4 NPs by solvothermal reduction and formed Fe-ICGCDs. A three-step reaction occurred in the preparation of magnetic Fe3O4 NPs, involving nucleation, crystal nucleation at the bubble-liquid interface, and particle development and assembly. Ethylene glycol as a mild reducing agent was used to reduce Fe (III) into Fe (II), hence, formatting Fe3O4 NPs. The SEM image showed that Fe-ICGCDs had uniformly spherical shapes with a particle size of ca.350 nm (Fig. 2C). After CS modification, the size of Fe-ICGCDs@CS slightly increased to 450 nm and had a smoother surface than unmodified Fe-ICGCDs (Figs. S1, 2D). The element mapping results showed a uniform distribution of O, Fe, C, S, Na, and N (Fig. 2E). The EDX maps showed that the contents of each element were as follows: O: 42.8 %; C: 31.6 %; Fe: 25.2 %; S: 0.2 %; Na: 0.1 %; N: 0.1 %.

The FTIR spectra of ICGCDs, Fe-ICGCDs, CS, and Fe-ICGCDs@CS are shown in Fig. 3A. The characteristic peak at 572 cm−1 was from the FeO bond vibration of Fe3O4 nanocrystals. The typical absorption peaks of CS were detected near 3430 cm−1, 2910 cm−1, and 1072 cm−1, which belong to the primary amine (NH), CH bond of the main polymer and CO bond of amide, respectively. The infrared spectra of Fe-ICGCDs@CS demonstrated that CS was successfully coated on Fe-ICGCDs nanospheres. The higher positive zeta potential value of Fe-ICGCDs@CS than Fe-ICGCDs indicated the coating of positively charged CS, which would be beneficial for nanomaterials electrostatically attaching to bacteria (Fig. S2). The UV–Vis-NIR spectra of different nanocomposite aqueous dispersions showed that the ICGCDs solution has natural absorption bands at 780 nm and 895 nm, indicating the formation of a larger conjugate structure during carbonization (Fig. 3B). The absorption peak at 780 nm is significantly lower than that of ICG alone. Fe-ICGCDs and Fe-ICGCDs@CS showed wide absorption in the range of 400–1000 nm, suggesting their great potential as photothermal agents in the NIR region.

The XRD patterns analysis showed Fe3O4 crystals (JCPD no. 65-3107) and a characteristic peak of CDs at 21°, which proved that the main body of Fe-ICGCDs@CS were Fe3O4 crystals and CDs (Fig. 3C). Then, the chemical composition of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was further characterized by XPS, which contained C (ca. 285.3 eV), O (ca. 527.6 eV), Fe (ca. 93.1 eV, 710.8 eV, and 723.2 eV), N (ca. 399.2 eV), S, Na, and C (Fig. 3D). As shown in Fig. 3E, five peaks for Fe were detected, including the primary peaks of Fe 2p3/2, and Fe 2p1/2 loaded at 709.1 eV and 722.9 eV, and three peaks identified as Fe2+ and Fe3+ by comparison with conventional binding energies. The magnetic characteristic of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was investigated using a hysteresis loop at room temperature (300 K) (Fig. 3F). Fe-ICGCDs@CS exhibited a typical superparamagnetic behavior, with a saturation magnetization of 59.80 emu/g, which was sufficient for magnetic separation. The magnetic adsorption behavior of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was observed with the application of magnetic field stresses, and the material was evenly dispersed and absorbed into the magnet within 2 min, which confirmed the magnetic properties of Fe-ICGCDs@CS (Fig. S3).

To explore the photothermal effect of Fe-ICGCDs@CS under 808 nm, deionized water was utilized as the negative control. The photothermal performance of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was assessed at various concentrations and power densities. Temperature changing curves showed that the temperature of Fe-ICGCDs@CS solution increased in a concentration-dependent manner and reached to 54.1 °C after 10 min at the concentration of 400 μg/mL, which was sufficient for further PTT application (Fig. 4A). In contrast, temperature in the control group increased to 29.4 °C after 10 min. Thermal images of Fe-ICGCDs@CS solution also revealed that the temperature of Fe-ICGCDs@CS increased with the increasing irradiation time (Fig. 4B). Besides, the photothermal property of Fe-ICGCDs@CS (400 μg/mL) was also recorded at different irradiation power densities, which showed that the temperature increased with the increasing irradiation power density (Fig. 4C). To investigate the photostability, Fe-ICGCDs@CS solution was irradiated under 808 nm laser for five “ON/OFF” cycles, and the temperature changes were also measured. The temperature curved showed no considerably diminish after five consecutive times of “ON/OFF” laser irradiation, demonstrating the excellent photothermal stability of Fe-ICGCDs@CS (Figs. 4D, S4). The photothermal conversion efficiency was estimated to be 35.5 % based on the previously reported formula (Figs. 4E, S5).

Due to the low systemic toxicity and modest invasions, PDT has gained considerable therapeutic effects against bacterial infections. To determine the photodynamic property of Fe-ICGCDs@CS under NIR laser irradiation, the generation of single oxygen (1O2) was detected by using 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) probes [46]. Fe-ICGCDs@CS was dissolved in ethanol (400 μg/mL) and mixed with 40 μL of DPBF (2 mM). The results indicated that absorbance intensities of DPBF in pure NIR and Fe-ICGCDs@CS groups had negligible change after 10 min (Fig. 5A, B). However, absorbance intensities of DPBF gradually decreased in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR group with increasing exposure duration, suggesting the production of 1O2 (Fig. 5C). Moreover, ESR analysis was further performed to assess the production of 1O2. As shown in Fig. 5D, no TEMP/1O2 signal was detected in the control group, while the TEMP/1O2 signals were apparently detected in ICGCDs and Fe-ICGCDs@CS groups. Notably, the stronger signal peaks in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS group than those in the pure ICGCDs group were attributed to the enhanced dispersion of CDs and the photodynamic properties of Fe3O4. These results confirmed that Fe-ICGCDs@CS could be used as photodynamic agents.

Considering the heavy need for PDT in O2 levels and hypoxia conditions within bacterial biofilms, nanomaterials that could produce O2 would be beneficial in overcoming the anoxic environment in the inflamed wound area. The O2 production capacity of different nanocomposites (400 μg/mL) was investigated by detecting the generation of bubbles with H2O2 substrate (1 M) (Fig. 5E). The results showed that O2 was continuously produced in a time-dependent manner and even lasted for 50 min. In particular, Fe-ICGCDs@CS generated visible bubbles at 0 min. The O2 production effect of different materials was quantified using dissolved O2 curves, which showed the best capability of Fe-ICGCDs (Fig. S6). Besides, the O2 production ability of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was also found related to the concentration of H2O2 (Fig. 5F).

The targeting performance of Fe-ICGCDs@CS on bacteria was investigated by observing the photoluminescence capabilities of ICGCDs using SYTO9 dye. As displayed in Fig. 6A, Escherichia coli (E. coli) in the control group showed a homogeneous rod shape and was stained by SYTO9 with bright green fluorescence, while the blue and red channels showed no fluorescence. It is noteworthy that the distribution of blue and red fluorescence in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS group was equal to that of green fluorescence and concentrated in bacteria. Blue and red fluorescence in the Fe-ICGCDs group was evenly distributed throughout the visual field. Similarly, Fe-ICGCDs@CS could also accumulate in Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), suggesting that CS modification contributed to the electrostatically targeting ability of Fe-ICGCDs@CS to bacteria (Fig. 6B).

To further investigate the in vitro antibacterial capacity of Fe-ICGCDs@CS, the production of ROS in bacteria was detected using a dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) probe. DCFH-DA could be hydrolyzed by intracellular esterase and form non-fluorescent DCFH, which could be oxidized into fluorescent DCF by ROS. As shown in Fig. 7A, H2O2 + NIR, and pure Fe-ICGCDs@CS groups showed no visible green fluorescence, while weak green fluorescence occurred in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 group, which might be because of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4. A bright green fluorescence was detected in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR group, indicating the better photodynamic activity of Fe-ICGCDs@CS than Fe3O4, which was beneficial for performing PDT in bacteria elimination.

Besides, the plate counting method was used to investigate the antibacterial ability of Fe-ICGCDs@CS. NIR refers to the 808 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2, 10 min), the concentration of H2O2 is 2 mM, and M refers to an external magnet, which was used to control the movement of Fe-ICGCDs@CS. The formed colonies in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 group were slightly inhibited compared to the control group, which was because of the generated ROS by Fe3O4 (Fig. 7B). In contrast, no significant change was observed in pure H2O2, NIR, and Fe-ICGCDs@CS groups. Notably, bacteria colonies sharply decreased in Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR and Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR groups, indicating the excellent antibacterial performance of Fe-ICGCDs@CS-induced PTT/PDT. As calculated, the relative activity of E. coli and S. aureus in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR group decreased by 99.63 % and 99.03 %, respectively (Fig. S7). A live/dead staining experiment was applied to evaluate the antibacterial effectiveness of Fe-ICGCDs@CS. Calcein-AM (green fluorescence) was used to stain living bacteria, whereas PI (red fluorescence) was used to stain the dead bacteria. Bacteria with Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR and Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatments displayed bright red fluorescence signals, while other groups had negligible red fluorescence (Fig. 7C), suggesting that the Fe-ICGCDs@CS-based PTT/PDT had excellent antibacterial effect under NIR laser irradiation.

To investigate the antibacterial mechanisms, SEM and TEM imaging were used to analyze the morphological changes of E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 8A). E. coli and S. aureus showed intact and smooth bacterial membranes in control, H2O2 + NIR, Fe-ICGCDs@CS, Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2, and Fe-ICGCDs@CS + M groups, indicating their negligible damage to bacteria. However, severe damage appeared with the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR treatment, which was because of the generated hyperthermia and 1O2-induced protein desaturation and oxidative stress. Bacterial morphology in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR group was mostly deformed with leaked contents, suggesting serious bacterial deaths. TEM images of E. coli and S. aureus in the control group showed intact cell boundaries, while blurred boundaries and cavities were detected in bacteria with Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatment, suggesting the excellent antibacterial ability of Fe-ICGCDs@CS-based PTT/PDT effect (Fig. 8B, C).

Bacterial biofilms often cause slow wound healing and chronic inflammation due to the extracellular matrix and colonization at wound sites. Fe3O4 is expected to be a proper substitute that could move controllably with an external magnetic field. Therefore, we investigated the effect of the magnetic ability of Fe-ICGCDs@CS on bacterial biofilms (Fig. 8D). E. coli and S. aureus (OD600 = 1.0) were stained with SYTO9/PI and detected using CLSM after different treatments. The intact and bright green fluorescence of bacteria at the bottom of containers in control and H2O2 + NIR groups indicated the successful formation of bacterial biofilms. With the addition of Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR, strong red fluorescence bacterial agglomeration was detected, which might be because of the electrostatic attraction of Fe-ICGCDs@CS. Meanwhile, slightly green fluorescence appeared in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 group, suggesting that controlling the movement of Fe-ICGCDs@CS in biofilms by an external magnet would contribute to reducing bacterial colonization, easily washing away during operation, and destroying the compact structure of biofilms. There was no green fluorescence with Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatment, revealing their excellent antibacterial effect.

The in vitro biosafety of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was evaluated in L929 cells by using MTT and live/dead staining assays. Cell viability among all samples maintained relatively high upon 80 % even with 400 μg/mL concentration of Fe-ICGCDs@CS, demonstrating the great biocompatibility of Fe-ICGCDs@CS (Fig. S8). Live/dead staining also showed that no red fluorescence was detected without irradiation, confirming the above result. In vivo biosafety of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was evaluated by recording the body weights and H&E staining of major organs after intravenous injection. As shown in Fig. S9, the body weights did not have significant change among different groups, suggesting the great in vivo biosafety of Fe-ICGCDs@CS. Besides, histological analysis of major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) showed no significant inflammatory lesions appeared in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS group compared with the control group (Fig. S10), suggesting that Fe-ICGCDs@CS did not cause in vivo chronic toxicity and could be considered an ideal candidate in the biomedical field.

The in vivo antimicrobial efficacy of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was investigated in S. aureus-infected wound mice models. The animal experiments were approved by the animal ethics of Nanjing Normal University. Female Balb/c mice (6 weeks, 18–21 g) were used to create a 0.9 cm diameter wound on the left side of the mice back. Then, 100 mL of S. aureus was used to construct an inflamed wound model. Mice were randomly divided into 7 groups, including 1) control; 2) H2O2 + NIR; 3) Fe-ICGCDs@CS; 4) Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2; 5) Fe-ICGCDs@CS + M; 6) Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR; 7) Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR. In the H2O2 + NIR group, 50 μL of PBS and 50 μL of H2O2 (2 mM) were dropped at wound sites and then irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 10 min. The whole wound-healing process was performed as exhibited in Fig. 9A. The in vivo photothermal property of Fe-ICGCDs@CS was first evaluated. The temperature in wound regions of mice with Fe-ICGCDs@CS administration quickly increased from 26.4 °C to 54.3 °C within 10 min under 808 nm laser exposure, which was sufficient for eliminating bacteria in the wound region (Fig. 9B). In contrast, the temperature in the control group only increased to 30.6 °C, demonstrating the excellent in vivo photothermal effect of Fe-ICGCDs@CS under NIR laser irradiation.

To visualize the wound-healing process, photographs of wounds were recorded after various treatments (Fig. 9C). On day 1, all groups displayed a certain degree of wound infection, indicating the successfully established S. aureus-infected wound models. There was no significant difference in wound size of all groups during the early four days, but a significant red inflammatory situation occurred. On day 9, a completely closed wound was displayed in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR group, while wounds still possessed obvious boundaries and epidermal defects among other groups. The therapeutic efficiency was further quantitatively evaluated by tracing wound areas with ImageJ software (Fig. 9D). The results showed that the wound area in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR group almost disappeared and was calculated to be 0.97 %, consistent with wound-bed closure trace results (Fig. 10A). These results suggested that Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatment could efficiently rescue the inflamed wound and promote the healing process.

Furthermore, the antibacterial mechanisms of Fe-ICGCDs@CS were evaluated by performing histological analyses. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson staining showed that wounds among groups (1)–(4) were not completely closed, while wounds among groups (5)–(7) formed granulation tissues (Fig. 10B). The infected wounds with Fe-ICGCDs@CS + NIR and Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatments possessed thinner granulation tissues and relatively thicker epidermis layers on day 9 compared with wounds in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + M group. In conclusion, the wounds of mice with Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatment formed a relatively complete organizational structure to act as a physical barrier against external infectious agents and mechanical injury. Simultaneously, Masson staining results demonstrated that the most tissue-structured aligned collagen (blue) formed with Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatment, indicating the generation of new tissues (Fig. 10C). These data confirmed that Fe-ICGCDs@CS mediated PTT/PDT synergistic strategy had a potent antibacterial effect and wound healing promotion.

Considering the critical role of cytokines in regulating cellular activity during the wound healing process, we performed immunohistochemistry assays to test the levels of inflammatory and repair cytokines, such as TNF-α and CD31. The level of CD31 increased in the Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR group, which indicated palingenetic angiogenesis (Fig. 10D). In contrast, the level of TNF-α apparently deceased after Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatment compared to other groups, demonstrating that PTT/PDT synergetic strategy could effectively suppress the early inflammatory responses at infected wound sites (Fig. 10E). The obtained results revealed the huge potential of Fe-ICGCDs@CS in wound healing promotion and anti-inflammation.

In summary, we report novel removable magnetic nanoplatforms, Fe-ICGCDs@CS, to generate the PTT/PDT effect for biofilm destruction and bioimaging. On the one hand, the excellent photothermal conversion efficiency of ICGCDs contributes to the required reduction of laser irradiation power, which could extremely avoid destroying the surrounding normal tissues. On the other hand, the controlled movement of Fe-ICGCDs@CS under an external magnetic field could enhance the penetration into biofilms, which is conducive to disrupting the compact structure of biofilms and bacterial colonization at the wound sites. Moreover, the live bacteria amount inside biofilms was greatly reduced after Fe-ICGCDs@CS + H2O2 + M + NIR treatment, effectively avoiding the inflammation and other adverse reactions caused by live bacteria. Taken together, the Fe-ICGCDs@CS-based PTT/PDT strategy provides a promising strategy for complete biofilm destruction and precise antibacterial therapy.

Yumeng Gao: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jing Wu: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Jian Shen: Resources, Methodology. Yan Xu: Resources, Formal analysis. Lu Li: Software, Resources. Wentao Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ninglin Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Conceptualization. Ming Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the Key Project of the Natural Science Foundation for Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BE2020347), Jiangsu Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Function Materials, Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials and Jiangsu Province Engineering Research Center of Stomatological Translational Medicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.129402.
